skill.name: github-oauth-nango-integration
skill.description: Guides implementation of GitHub OAuth and GitHub App authentication with Nango using separate login and repository access connections.
skill.summary: Implement GitHub login and GitHub App repository access with Nango.
skill.author: AgentWorkforce

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution alerts, but they are false positives from Markdown backticks, tables, and TypeScript examples. The skill is documentation for an OAuth integration, not executable code, but it includes security-sensitive examples for tokens, webhooks, network calls, and environment secrets that require caution.

medium_findings.title: JWT Stored In Browser Local Storage
medium_findings.description: The frontend example stores an issued token in localStorage. This is vulnerable to token theft if the application has an XSS flaw, so production code should prefer HttpOnly secure cookies or another protected storage pattern.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sample explicitly writes result.token to localStorage after a login flow. This is a common and concrete security risk for browser authentication tokens.

medium_findings.title: Webhook Signature Example Lacks Hardened Verification Details
medium_findings.description: The webhook sample computes an HMAC and compares it with a direct string inequality. It does not show timing-safe comparison, replay protection, or timestamp validation, which are important for production webhook handling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code shows direct comparison of the received signature and expected signature. The surrounding section is explicitly about webhook signature verification.

medium_findings.title: Security-Sensitive Secrets And Repository Permissions
medium_findings.description: The instructions reference Nango secrets, JWT secrets, database URLs, GitHub private keys, and repository scopes. This is legitimate for the integration, but users must enforce least privilege and secret handling controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill directly instructs users to configure multiple secrets and GitHub App permissions. This is expected for OAuth setup, but it increases operational risk if implemented carelessly.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The many external command findings are caused by inline code, tables, fenced code blocks, and route examples. No evidence found of executable shell commands being invoked by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation examples and Markdown formatting. They do not define runnable scripts or command execution hooks.

low_findings.title: Network Calls Are First-Party Integration Examples
low_findings.description: The fetch examples call local application routes, and the webhook URL is a placeholder domain. No evidence found of data exfiltration to an untrusted external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are relative routes or an example placeholder. The context is normal frontend and webhook setup for Nango.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: The static LLM role token alert appears to match ordinary constant names and authentication terms. No evidence found of instructions that tell the model to ignore policies or change the audit result.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited section only defines GitHub integration constants. It contains no system override language or marketplace review bypass claim.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Is Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography alert is not supported by the cited front matter. The actual HMAC example uses SHA-256, which is not a weak hash for this purpose.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is descriptive metadata, not cryptographic code. The only shown signature algorithm is HMAC-SHA256.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser Token Persistence
dangerous_patterns.description: The example persists a login token in localStorage, which can expose authentication tokens after client-side script compromise.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line directly stores the returned token in localStorage. The risk depends on application XSS resistance, but the pattern is clearly present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Direct Signature String Comparison
dangerous_patterns.description: The webhook verification sample compares signatures with a direct inequality instead of showing a timing-safe comparison.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The HMAC verification code is visible and uses a direct comparison. This is a recognized hardening gap in webhook implementations.

content.value_statement: GitHub login and repository authorization often need separate OAuth flows. This skill shows a two-connection Nango pattern for user identity, GitHub App access, and webhook processing.
actual_capabilities: Explains the separate Nango connections for GitHub user login and GitHub App repository access.
Provides TypeScript-style examples for route handlers, webhook processing, and frontend connect flows.
Outlines database tables for users, repositories, and GitHub App installations.
Shows how to handle returning users, pending organization approval, and repository sync status.
Lists Nango dashboard setup steps, required secrets, scopes, and webhook configuration.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not include a complete runnable application.
Security examples need production hardening before deployment.
It assumes Nango, GitHub OAuth, and GitHub App concepts are already available in the project.
It does not cover every framework, database adapter, or GitHub permission model.

use_cases.title: Add GitHub Login To A SaaS App
use_cases.description: Use the login flow guidance to create Nango sessions, process auth webhooks, and issue application tokens.

use_cases.title: Connect User Repositories Through A GitHub App
use_cases.description: Use the repo access flow to link GitHub App installations to authenticated users and synced repositories.

use_cases.title: Review OAuth Architecture Before Implementation
use_cases.description: Use the flow diagrams and common mistakes to validate separation between identity, repository access, and webhooks.

prompt_templates.title: Understand The Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how this skill separates GitHub user login from GitHub App repository access. List the main data records I need.

prompt_templates.title: Map Routes To My Backend
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me adapt the Nango GitHub OAuth routes from this skill to my existing backend framework and auth middleware.

prompt_templates.title: Design Webhook Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design my Nango webhook handler. Include auth events, sync events, error paths, and signature verification hardening.

prompt_templates.title: Audit A Full Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my GitHub OAuth and GitHub App implementation against this skill. Check connection IDs, repo ownership, pending approval handling, secrets, and token storage.

output_examples.input: I need GitHub login and repository access in one product.
output: Use the GitHub OAuth connection for user identity.
Use the GitHub App OAuth connection for repository permissions.
Link both flows through the authenticated user record and Nango connection IDs.

output_examples.input: My user installed the GitHub App for an organization, but repos are not available yet.
output_examples.output: The expected result is a pending approval state. Store the pending installation request, show a waiting message, and retry after organization approval.

output_examples.input: I am preparing the Nango webhook endpoint for production.
output: Verify the Nango signature before parsing business events.
Use timing-safe comparison and replay protection.
Route auth events and sync events through separate handlers.

best_practices: Keep user identity and repository access in separate Nango connections.
Use least-privilege GitHub App permissions and rotate all secrets through a managed secret store.
Harden webhook verification and avoid storing browser tokens in localStorage for production systems.
anti_patterns: Using one GitHub connection for both login identity and repository access.
Trusting Nango or GitHub webhook payloads before verifying their signatures.
Leaving temporary connection IDs or duplicate connections active after returning user login.

faq.question: Why does the skill use two Nango integrations?
faq.answer: GitHub OAuth identifies the user. A GitHub App grants repository access. The skill links both through the user record.

faq.question: Does this skill include working application code?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation guidance and examples that must be adapted to your stack.

faq.question: Can I use this with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is text-based and can be used with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: What should I change before production?
faq.answer: Harden token storage, webhook verification, secret management, logging, error handling, and GitHub App permissions.

faq.question: How does it handle organization approval delays?
faq.answer: The example stores a pending installation request when GitHub returns a permission error during repository access.

faq.question: Does it access my GitHub data by itself?
faq.answer: No. The skill is documentation. Any access happens only in code you implement and authorize.
